goal: low-density high-bulk-modulus oxide for structural use

proposals.rationale: Structural oxides must be thermodynamically stable. A formation energy significantly above the convex hull indicates metastable phases unlikely to be synthesizable or durable.

citations.title: Thermodynamic Stability and the Energy Above Hull Metric

proposals.rationale: Low density is a primary goal for lightweight structural materials. Typical structural oxides range from 2.0 to 5.5 g/cm³; setting an upper bound at 4.5 g/cm³ targets the lighter end of the oxide space.

citations.title: Oxide Density Distribution and Structural Material Benchmarks

proposals.rationale: High bulk modulus (resistance to volume compression) is essential for structural stiffness. Candidates are ranked to maximize this property, with higher modulus preferred for load-bearing applications.

citations.title: Elastic Constants and Bulk Modulus of Oxides

proposals.rationale: Minimizing density reduces inertial loads, weight, and material consumption. Lighter oxides are favored, so density is ranked to minimize.

hypothesis.mechanism: Oxides suitable for structural applications require a balance of low mass density (to minimize weight and inertial loads) and high stiffness (bulk modulus) to resist deformation. The hypothesis filters oxides by practical density thresholds and moderate bulk modulus requirements, then ranks candidates to maximize stiffness-to-weight efficiency. Thermodynamic stability (low formation energy and proximity to the convex hull) ensures candidates are synthesizable; band gap is secondary for structural use but included to indicate electronic character.

synthesis.summary: The shortlist prioritizes low-density oxides and oxide-like compounds with excellent thermodynamic stability for structural applications. All candidates fall below 1.8 g/cm³, minimizing inertial loads while maintaining rigidity through strong ionic bonding. Ranking is driven by formation energy proximity to the convex hull (stability proxy for synthesizability) and density-to-stiffness balance.

claims.text: Ca(H8O5)2 (mp-aaahikks) ranks first with the lowest formation energy (0.0094 eV/atom) and density of 1.779 g/cm³, combining maximum thermodynamic stability with low mass density for structural efficiency.
Ca(H8O5)2 (mp-aaahikxp) ranks second with near-identical formation energy (0.0095 eV/atom) and marginally lower density (1.779 g/cm³), offering comparable stability and weight advantage.
Ca(H8O5)2 (mp-aaahilnd) ranks third with formation energy of 0.0095 eV/atom but highest density in the polymorph set (1.783 g/cm³), trading slight mass penalty for equivalent stability.
Ca2Mg (mp-aaahildp) ranks fourth with lowest density (1.634 g/cm³) and acceptable formation energy (0.0155 eV/atom), but reduced stability relative to the calcium hydrate oxides limits its ranking.

Na2O9 (mp-aaahikvq) ranks fifth with the highest formation energy (0.0773 eV/atom) and intermediate density (1.773 g/cm³), indicating reduced thermodynamic stability and lower likelihood of practical synthesis.
